target.title: fix(agents): resolve bare alias fallbacks via alias index
target.body:
## Summary

Fix Telegram/Discord/Mattermost `/model` pickers misclassifying configured CLI-runtime bare aliases (`opus-cli`, `sonnet-cli`, etc.) under the agent's primary provider.

`buildAllowedModelSetWithFallbacks` (`src/agents/model-selection-shared.ts`) was synthesizing a fake `<defaultProvider>/<aliasName>` catalog entry for any bare-name fallback because `addAllowedModelRef` did not consult the configured alias index. Repro: a config with `model.fallbacks: ["opus-cli", ...]` and `models["claude-cli/claude-opus-4-6"].alias = "opus-cli"` made `opus-cli` appear under `openai-codex` (the primary provider) in `/model`.

The fix builds the alias index up front in `buildAllowedModelSetWithFallbacks` and short-circuits bare-name resolution through `aliasIndex.byAlias` before falling through to existing `resolveBareModelDefaultProvider` logic. Alias-first lookup is scoped to the `fallbackModels` loop only (not the explicit `agents.defaults.models` allowlist keys) so a bare allowlist key whose string happens to match another entry's alias is not silently rewritten — addresses Codex P2 review.

Resolved CLI-runtime aliases hit the existing `isModelPickerVisibleProvider` filter and are hidden from the picker (option a — conservative). Canonicalising CLI-runtime refs to the canonical provider with a runtime selector (option b) is the design-correct end state but deferred — needs a coordinated answer across all CLI backends and interacts with the runtime selector UX.

Side effect of the same synthesis bug: `allowedKeys` in `getModelRefStatusWithFallbackModels` was getting the wrong key (`openai-codex/opus-cli`) instead of the resolved one (`claude-cli/claude-opus-4-6`), so this is not just a UI fix — it also corrects the routing-allowlist gate.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Telegram/Discord/Mattermost `/models` picker incorrectly listing bare CLI-runtime aliases (e.g. `opus-cli`) under the agent's primary canonical provider (e.g. `openai-codex`), and the same misrouting in the `getModelRefStatusWithFallbackModels` allowlist gate.

**Real environment tested**: Local repo running the actual production picker-data path (`buildModelsProviderData` from `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.ts:68` — the same function consumed by Telegram/Discord/Mattermost `/models` commands) and the routing-allowlist gate (`getModelRefStatusWithFallbackModels` from `src/agents/model-selection-shared.ts:781`) directly against the bug-report config and a regression-witness config across three commits on this branch.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `pnpm exec tsx <script>` where the script calls `buildModelsProviderData(cfg)` and `getModelRefStatusWithFallbackModels(...)` against the configs below, on each of `ffafa9008d` (main pre-PR), `5e545005f4` (initial PR fix), and the current PR tip.

```ts
// bug-report cfg (from the gist)
{ agents: { defaults: {
  model: { primary: "openai-codex/gpt-5.5", fallbacks: ["opus-cli", "anthropic/claude-opus-4-6"] },
  models: {
    "openai-codex/gpt-5.5": {},
    "claude-cli/claude-opus-4-6": { alias: "opus-cli" },
    "anthropic/claude-opus-4-6": {},
  },
}}}

// regression-witness cfg (Codex P2 scenario)
{ agents: { defaults: {
  model: { primary: "openai/gpt-4o" },
  models: {
    "openai/gpt-4o": {},
    "gpt-5.5": {},
    "claude-cli/claude-opus-4-6": { alias: "gpt-5.5" },
  },
}}}
```

**Evidence after fix**: terminal output, copied live from a Node process running the actual `buildModelsProviderData` and `getModelRefStatusWithFallbackModels` runtime against the configs above on the PR tip:

```
=== bug-report cfg (primary openai-codex/gpt-5.5, fallbacks include 'opus-cli') ===
providers: [ 'anthropic', 'openai-codex' ]
byProvider: {
  "anthropic": [ "claude-opus-4-6" ],
  "openai-codex": [ "gpt-5.5" ]
}
openai-codex contains 'opus-cli'? false
claude-cli present as provider? false

=== regression cfg (bare key 'gpt-5.5' + another entry alias = 'gpt-5.5') ===
providers: [ 'openai' ]
byProvider: {
  "openai": [ "gpt-4o", "gpt-5.5" ]
}

=== routing-allowlist gate (Codex P2 regression scenario) ===
openai/gpt-5.5 (bare allowlist key, not the default) allowed? true (must be true)
```

Before fix (`ffafa9008d`, main) — original bug visible in same runtime:

```
=== bug-report cfg ===
byProvider: {
  "openai-codex": [ "gpt-5.5", "opus-cli" ]   <-- leaked alias
}
openai-codex contains 'opus-cli'? true
```

Before fix (`5e545005f4`, initial PR fix) — Codex P2 regression visible:

```
=== regression cfg ===
byProvider: { "openai": [ "gpt-4o" ] }   <-- bare 'gpt-5.5' allowlist key dropped
=== routing-allowlist gate ===
openai/gpt-5.5 allowed? false   <-- regression
```

**Observed result after fix**: At PR tip, the `byProvider` map produced by the live `buildModelsProviderData` runtime no longer contains `opus-cli` under `openai-codex` for the bug-report config, and `getModelRefStatusWithFallbackModels` correctly returns `allowed: true` for `openai/gpt-5.5` in the regression-witness config. Both pre-existing failing states (original bug + Codex P2 regression) are gone on the tip.

**What was not tested**: Live end-to-end Telegram bot interaction (sending `/models openai-codex` from a real Telegram chat to a running gateway) was not run, because the proof exercises the same shared `buildModelsProviderData` function the channel handler consumes — channel plugin button rendering is a pure passthrough of the `byProvider` map captured above.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/agents/model-selection.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.test.ts` → all green.
- New regression test in `src/agents/model-selection.test.ts` deterministically reproduces the original bug AND the Codex P2 regression: confirmed failing on `main` pre-fix and on `5e545005f4` (initial PR fix) respectively, passing on the PR tip.
- New integration test in `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-models.test.ts` asserts `byProvider.get("openai-codex")` does not contain `"opus-cli"` for the bug-report config.
- Existing `commands-models.test.ts` "deepseek-v4-flash" test still passes (catalog-inference fallback path preserved for non-aliased bare names).
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` on touched files → clean.
- `pnpm check:changed` → all gates pass.

## Cross-surface impact

`buildAllowedModelSetWithFallbacks` is shared. Fix benefits: Telegram `/model`, Discord model picker, Mattermost model picker, and any caller of `buildModelsProviderData` / `getModelRefStatusWithFallbackModels`.

## Related

- #75267 fixes the same symptom in the interactive CLI/onboard flow (`src/flows/model-picker.ts`) only. This PR addresses the shared seam used by channel surfaces. Files do not overlap; the two land independently.

## Test plan

- [x] Failing seam unit test exercises bug-report config; passes after fix.
- [x] Failing seam unit test for Codex P2 regression scenario; passes after fix.
- [x] Integration test through `buildModelsProviderData` confirms `openai-codex` provider list does not contain bare CLI-runtime aliases.
- [x] Full `model-selection.test.ts` + `commands-models.test.ts` suites pass post-fix.
- [x] `oxfmt --check` clean on touched files.
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` clean.

target.title: fix(proxy): apply enhanced NO_PROXY matching to global undici dispatcher (fixes #95998)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #95998.

When `HTTPS_PROXY` is set, the global EnvHttpProxyAgent routes ALL requests through the proxy — including internal plugin requests that should bypass via `NO_PROXY`. The built-in undici NO_PROXY matcher does not support leading-dot subdomain patterns, CIDR notation, or octet wildcards.

OpenClaw already has `matchesNoProxy()` that handles these — but it was only used by provider helpers, not the global dispatcher.

## Changes Made

1. **NO_PROXY-aware proxy dispatcher** — Wraps every EnvHttpProxyAgent with a Proxy that checks `matchesNoProxy()` before dispatching. Bypassed requests are routed through a direct Agent with the same timeout and connect policy.

2. **Lifecycle synchronization (P1 fix)** — Promise-style `close()`/`destroy()` now **await** both the bypass agent AND the proxy dispatcher before settling. Previously the bypass agent was invoked fire-and-forget, so awaited global dispatcher shutdown could complete before bypass sockets were drained.

3. **Timeout/connect consistency** — `forceResetGlobalDispatcher` now passes `connect` options to the bypass agent, matching other construction paths.

## Evidence

**Unit tests (35 tests):**
```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  35 passed (35)
```

**CIDR and octet-wildcard support (undici built-in v8.5.0 lacks these):**
```
=== Enhanced NO_PROXY matcher ===

  ✅ CIDR: 100.64.0.0/10 match: matchesNoProxy=true
  ✅ CIDR: outside range: matchesNoProxy=false
  ✅ CIDR: 169.254.0.0/16 match: matchesNoProxy=true
  ✅ CIDR: 169.254.0.0/16 no match: matchesNoProxy=false
  ✅ Octet wildcard: 100.100.*.*: matchesNoProxy=true
  ✅ Octet wildcard: no match: matchesNoProxy=false
  ✅ Subdomain: bucket.cos.myqcloud.com (.myqcloud.com): match=true
  ✅ Subdomain: api.openai.com (.myqcloud.com): match=false
```

**Real proxy live proof:**
```
=== Without NO_PROXY ===
  [proxy] CONNECT httpbin.org:443 → through proxy ✅

=== NO_PROXY.myqcloud.com ===
  COS domain → 0 proxy connections (bypassed) ✅

=== Non-matching domain ===
  api.openai.com → through proxy (35 CONNECTs) ✅
```

## AI Assistance

- AI-assisted: Yes

target.title: fix(agents): xAI/Grok requests fail after a stale reasoning replay ("could not decrypt encrypted_content")
target.body:
Closes #97925

_AI-assisted (Claude Code); diagnosed from production gateway logs (see Evidence)._

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users routing reasoning-enabled xAI/Grok (`grok-4.3`) requests through the gateway hit a **full `grok-4.3` outage**: a stale reasoning replay makes xAI return `400 "Could not decrypt the provided encrypted_content…"`, the request fails, and the per-model circuit breaker opens — so **every** subsequent `grok-4.3` call fails with `Circuit breaker open for xai/grok-4.3` until someone intervenes. Any agent or channel on a Grok route is affected.

## Why This Change Was Made

openclaw already recovers from a stale reasoning replay — it strips `encrypted_content` and retries once (`stripResponsesRequestEncryptedContent`). That recovery is gated on `isInvalidEncryptedContentError()`, which only recognized the `invalid_encrypted_content` / `thinking_signature_invalid` codes and messages. xAI/Grok returns a **code-less** prose 400, so the matcher returned `false` and the request failed instead of retrying.

This adds one message match — `encrypted_content` present **and** a decrypt-failure phrase — so the existing strip-and-retry handles xAI too. It is kept deliberately narrow: unrelated "could not decrypt" messages that don't mention `encrypted_content` (e.g. the OAuth-sidecar warning) are excluded, and a negative test asserts that. Blast radius is contained: `isInvalidEncryptedContentError` has a **single caller** (the one-shot retry guard), the retry is **bounded to one attempt**, and it only strips `encrypted_content` — so even a hypothetical over-match costs at most one extra request before the original error surfaces.

## User Impact

xAI/Grok reasoning requests now self-recover from a stale reasoning replay (one transparent retry with `encrypted_content` stripped) instead of failing and tripping the per-model breaker. No more gateway-wide `grok-4.3` outages from this cause, and it survives future xAI model-snapshot rotations. No behavior change for other providers unless they emit the same recoverable error. No UI change.

## Evidence

- **New unit test, red→green.** Mirrors the existing `thinking_signature_invalid` retry test. On clean `main` it fails (no retry); with the fix it passes:
  - `× retries xAI's code-less 'could not decrypt encrypted_content' 400 …` — fails on `main`
  - passes with the fix.
- **Negative test** asserts an unrelated `"could not decrypt"` message (no `encrypted_content`) does **not** retry — proving the match is narrow.
- **oxlint** clean on both files.
- **Validation scope:** ran the affected vitest file (`src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`) + oxlint locally; relying on CI for the full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` lane.
- **Production logs (redacted)** that motivated this — 92 occurrences over ~8h until the session's reasoning state was cleared:
  ```
  [openai-transport] [responses] error provider=xai api=openai-responses model=grok-4.3 status=400 code=undefined message=400 "Could not decrypt the provided encrypted_content. Ensure the value is the unmodified encrypted_content from a previous response."
  res ✗ agent errorMessage=FallbackSummaryError: All models failed (1): xai/grok-4.3: Circuit breaker open for xai/grok-4.3
  ```

target.title: fix(google): bound Gemini batch embedding file content reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## Summary

- Replaces Gemini batch embedding output download parsing with streaming JSONL parsing instead of whole-file `res.text()` buffering.
- Allows valid batch output files larger than the generic 16 MiB text cap while bounding each individual JSONL record to 16 MiB.
- Intentionally out of scope: changing Gemini batch submission/polling behavior or adding live Gemini credentials.
- Review focus: the intentional per-record cap and the streamed output parsing strategy.

## Linked context

Related context: #97687.

Related: provider response hardening series for successful provider/control-plane reads.

Maintainer request: not directly requested by a maintainer.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Gemini embedding batch output files were previously read with unbounded whole-body text parsing, which can OOM for large JSONL output files.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Node 22 using `runGeminiEmbeddingBatches` against a real `node:http` server bound to `127.0.0.1`; no fetch mock on the streaming proof cases.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/google/embedding-batch.test.ts -- --run -t "file-content streaming"`
- Evidence after fix:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  2 passed | 4 skipped (6)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard
```

- Observed result after fix: a valid local JSONL output file with 16,500 embedding records and total size above 16 MiB streams successfully; a single oversized JSONL record rejects with `gemini.batch-file-content: JSONL line exceeds 16777216 bytes`.
- What was not tested: a live Gemini batch run with real Google credentials.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: no live Gemini API key is available in this environment. The proof exercises the production batch runner and real HTTP transport locally, including upload/create/download routing.
- Before evidence: source on `main` downloaded the successful output file as one full text body before parsing JSONL.

## Tests and validation

- `PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/google/embedding-batch.test.ts -- --run -t "file-content streaming"`
- Existing bounded JSON response tests for upload/create/status paths remain in the same file.
- Regression coverage proves both desired outcomes: total output larger than 16 MiB succeeds, but one oversized record fails closed.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. A single JSONL output record above 16 MiB now fails closed.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes, provider response streaming/size enforcement changed.

What is the highest-risk area? A provider-valid single embedding record above 16 MiB would now fail.

How is that risk mitigated? Normal embedding records are far below 16 MiB, and large total batch output remains supported by streaming line-by-line instead of capping the whole file.

## Current review state

Next action: ClawSweeper/maintainer re-review after this template/proof refresh.

Waiting on: maintainer acceptance of the 16 MiB per-JSONL-record guard or request for a different record cap.

Bot/reviewer comments addressed: the body now states that the cap is per line, not whole file, and documents the missing live Gemini proof as an environment limitation.

_AI-assisted; implementation and validation reviewed before pushing._

target.title: fix(ollama): preserve custom memory provider endpoints
target.body:
Closes #97055

## What Problem This Solves

Users who set `memorySearch.provider` to a custom Ollama-backed provider (e.g. `ollama-cpu` with its own `baseUrl`) had memory embeddings routed through the base `ollama` provider/endpoint instead of the configured one. The gateway acknowledged the requested provider in logs but connected to whichever Ollama instance resolved first on the network.

AI-assisted: this PR was prepared with Codex and reviewed with Claude.

## Why This Change Was Made

There were two layers dropping the configured provider id:

1. The gateway `/v1/embeddings` **memory-specific** adapter path did not pass the configured provider id into the adapter (only the generic path did). `embeddings-http.ts` now forwards `provider: providerId`, matching the generic path.
2. The Ollama memory embedding adapter hardcoded `provider: "ollama"`, discarding the caller's id. It now preserves the resolved id so `resolveConfiguredProvider` can find the custom `baseUrl`/headers, and records `baseUrl`/header identity in `cacheKeyData` so a custom endpoint does not silently share the base provider's memory index identity.

**Secret-header hardening (addresses the ClawSweeper P1 finding):** because `cacheKeyData` is hashed into the memory provider key, any credential header placed in it would force a full reindex on credential rotation. The Ollama adapter originally excluded only `Authorization`, diverging from the OpenAI-compatible adapter which treats `api-key`/`token`/`secret`/`proxy-authorization` as sensitive. The sensitive-header predicate is now centralized in the shared `memory-host-sdk` helper (`isSensitiveEmbeddingHeaderName`), so `sanitizeEmbeddingCacheHeaders` always drops credential headers for **every** embedding adapter, and the OpenAI-compatible adapter is migrated onto it (removing the duplicate). Secret custom headers are still sent on the wire — only the cache identity excludes them.

No new config surface is added.

## User Impact

Users can dedicate memory embeddings to a configured Ollama endpoint such as `ollama-cpu` without requests falling back to the default `ollama` endpoint, and memory indexing distinguishes custom endpoint identity instead of reusing vectors from a different endpoint.

**Compatibility note:** adding `baseUrl`/`headers` to the Ollama memory cache identity changes the provider-key hash for existing Ollama memory indexes (including the default `ollama` provider), so they will rebuild once via the normal memory index rebuild flow on upgrade. This is an intentional, maintainer-acceptance item — it is required to distinguish endpoints, and memory indexes are rebuildable. Credential-style headers do **not** participate in this identity, so rotating a secret header no longer churns the index.

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed:** custom `models.providers.<id>.api = "ollama"` memory providers keep their provider id through the gateway and Ollama memory adapter; secret custom headers stay out of cache identity.

**Real environment tested:** local source checkout with focused Vitest shards and mocked HTTP/provider boundaries.

**Exact steps:**

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  extensions/ollama/src/embedding-provider.test.ts \
  src/gateway/embeddings-http.test.ts \
  src/plugins/openai-compatible-embedding-provider.test.ts \
  extensions/memory-core/src/memory/embeddings.test.ts
git diff --check
```

**Evidence after fix:** all focused shards pass (Ollama provider, gateway embeddings HTTP, OpenAI-compatible provider, memory-core embeddings). Red/green verified by reverting the two source files to `main` with the new tests in place: the gateway regression fails (adapter receives no `ollama-cpu` provider) and the Ollama identity tests fail on the missing `baseUrl`; both pass with the fix. The OpenAI-compatible provider suite passes unchanged, confirming the predicate dedup is behavior-preserving. `pnpm tsgo:prod`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`, oxlint, and oxfmt are clean. `git diff --check` passes.

**Observed result:** the new Ollama regression sends `ollama-cpu` embeddings (including a custom `X-Api-Key` header) to the configured base URL, records provider/baseUrl/non-secret-header cache identity, and excludes `X-Api-Key` from `cacheKeyData`; the gateway regression proves configured custom provider ids reach memory-specific adapters.

**What was not tested:** no live two-Ollama deployment was run — the routing is proven at the unit level by asserting the outgoing request URL/headers and the gateway adapter call args, which is the deterministic seam. A live two-endpoint setup is still the contributor/maintainer real-behavior gate.


---

**Runtime proof — captured before/after (negative-control harness):**

Beyond the unit shards above, the fix was exercised at runtime with two local `node:http` servers standing in for two Ollama endpoints (the real external boundary), driving the actual on-disk `extensions/ollama/src/memory-embedding-adapter.ts` + `createOllamaEmbeddingProvider` + the real SSRF fetch guard via `tsx`. The same harness was run on unfixed `main` (negative control) and on this branch, under Node v22.22.3.

_Before — unfixed `main` (exit 1), the bug signature:_

```
provider "ollama-cpu" configured :53187  ->  NEITHER fake server — misrouted (likely default :11434)
    connect error: fetch failed (cause: ECONNREFUSED :11434)
provider "ollama-gpu" configured :53188  ->  NEITHER fake server — misrouted (likely default :11434)
distinct identity (no cache collision)? NO
RESULT: FAIL — misroute to the default :11434 endpoint and collapse to one cache identity.
```

_After — this branch (exit 0):_

```
provider "ollama-cpu" configured :53175  ->  OWN endpoint :53175
provider "ollama-gpu" configured :53176  ->  OWN endpoint :53176
X-Api-Key received by endpoint A: "super-secret-cpu-key" (sent — correct)
cacheKeyData (ollama-cpu): {"provider":"ollama-cpu","baseUrl":"http://127.0.0.1:53175","model":"qwen3-embedding:4b","headers":[["Content-Type","application/json"],["X-Ollama-Tenant","cpu"]]}
distinct identity (no cache collision)? YES
secret header (X-Api-Key) excluded from cache identity? YES
RESULT: PASS — custom Ollama memory endpoints are preserved and isolated.
```

The Ollama wire boundary is mocked (two `node:http` servers speaking the real `/api/embed` contract); the misroute in the negative control is a genuine network fact (a live `ECONNREFUSED` against the default :11434), and the fixed run delivers `X-Api-Key` over a real socket to the correct endpoint while excluding it from `cacheKeyData`. (Ports are assigned dynamically per run.)

target.title: fix(matrix): bound raw transport response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## Summary

- Bounds Matrix raw transport reads with the existing SDK response cap when callers omit `maxBytes`.
- Preserves explicit caller limits and now forwards encrypted-media `maxBytes/readIdleTimeoutMs` through the crypto facade into `MatrixClient.downloadContent`.
- Moves encrypted media download before native decrypt binding load so oversized media fails at the transport guard before decrypt setup.
- Review focus: Matrix media compatibility and the default 64 MiB cap for omitted raw reads.

## Linked context

Related context: #97644.

Related: Matrix media/download hardening and provider response-size guard series.

Maintainer request: not directly requested by a maintainer.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Matrix raw responses with omitted `maxBytes` could buffer unbounded data; encrypted media options were also dropped before transport.
- Real environment tested: local Matrix extension tests on Node 22 using real HTTP transport paths and a real `node:http` server for raw response cap proof.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.test.ts -- --run -t "preserves encrypted media download limits through the crypto facade"`
- Evidence after fix:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  1 passed | 84 skipped (85)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard
```

Earlier focused transport proof on this branch:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.test.ts -- --run
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  16 passed (16)
```

- Observed result after fix: encrypted media download limits reach the transport layer and reject oversized media with `Matrix media exceeds configured size limit`; raw omitted-`maxBytes` reads use the SDK default cap instead of `response.arrayBuffer()`.
- What was not tested: a live Matrix homeserver media download with private room media.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: proof is local transport/runtime proof with deterministic HTTP servers and SDK tests, not a live Matrix server capture.
- Before evidence: the crypto facade called `downloadContent(mxcUrl)` without forwarding opts, and raw omitted-`maxBytes` transport reads were unbounded.

## Tests and validation

- `PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.test.ts -- --run -t "preserves encrypted media download limits through the crypto facade"`
- `PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.test.ts -- --run`
- `/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules/.bin/oxlint extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/crypto-facade.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.test.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.test.ts`
- Regression coverage includes default raw cap, real HTTP oversized raw response rejection, under-cap raw success, and encrypted media option forwarding.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes. Oversized omitted-limit raw Matrix responses now fail closed.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes, Matrix response body handling is stricter.

What is the highest-risk area? A legitimate raw Matrix response above 64 MiB with omitted `maxBytes` now fails instead of buffering unbounded data.

How is that risk mitigated? Callers with a different limit can still pass explicit `maxBytes`; encrypted media configured limits are now preserved instead of dropped.

## Current review state

Next action: ClawSweeper/maintainer re-review after code follow-up and body proof refresh.

Waiting on: maintainer acceptance of the omitted-raw-read default cap and local proof in place of live Matrix media proof.

Bot/reviewer comments addressed: forwarded encrypted-media `maxBytes/readIdleTimeoutMs` into `MatrixClient.downloadContent` and added focused regression coverage for that path.

_AI-assisted; implementation and validation reviewed before pushing._

target.title: fix(feishu): bound Feishu API JSON response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a shared Feishu JSON response wrapper that delegates to `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http` while capping credentialed Feishu API JSON bodies at 16 MiB.
- Routes app-registration JSON reads through the shared helper, covering init, device-code begin, polling, and app-owner lookup.
- Extends the same cap to the sibling streaming-card token and card-create JSON reads, addressing the previous review finding about an uncovered Feishu credentialed path.
- Rebases cleanly onto latest `upstream/main` and keeps the upstream scan-to-create QR terminal rendering test.

## Linked context

No linked issue; this is a bounded-response-read hardening follow-up for Feishu/Lark credentialed API JSON responses.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Feishu app-registration and streaming-card credentialed API paths previously had successful JSON reads that could buffer an unbounded response body.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux x86-64, local OpenClaw repo environment, real `ReadableStream`/`Response` body paths through production functions after rebasing on 2026-06-29.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules \
    node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/app-registration.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.test.ts -- --run
  PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules \
    /media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules/.bin/oxlint \
    extensions/feishu/src/app-registration.ts extensions/feishu/src/app-registration.test.ts \
    extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.ts extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.test.ts \
    extensions/feishu/src/json-response.ts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```text
  [feishu fetchFeishuJson bound proof] over-cap: bytes_pulled=17825792 cap=16777216 canceled=true
  [feishu fetchFeishuJson bound proof] under-cap: returned={"deviceCode":"dev-code-123",...}
  [feishu streaming-card bound proof] token over-cap: bytes_pulled=17825855 cap=16777216 canceled=true
  [feishu streaming-card bound proof] card-create over-cap: bytes_pulled=17825855 cap=16777216 canceled=true
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests  26 passed (26)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** App-registration oversized JSON rejects with `feishu.api: JSON response exceeds ...`; malformed JSON is labelled `feishu.api`; streaming-card token and card-create oversized JSON reject with their own call-site labels and cancel the stream before reading the full body. Normal under-cap app-registration and streaming-card flows still parse and pass existing tests.
- **What was not tested:** Live Feishu/Lark tenant calls with real app credentials. No tenant credentials are available in this environment.
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** The proof uses real `ReadableStream` and `Response` body reads through production code but mocks `fetchWithSsrFGuard` to avoid live credentials and external network dependency.
- **Before evidence:** Previous branch was conflicting against main and only covered app-registration. The sibling `streaming-card.ts` token reader remained a bare JSON reader before this update.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/app-registration.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.test.ts -- --run` — 2 files, 26 tests passed.
- `oxlint extensions/feishu/src/app-registration.ts extensions/feishu/src/app-registration.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.ts extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/json-response.ts` — 0 problems.
- Merge conflict with the upstream QR rendering test resolved; that test remains in this branch.

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? **Only for oversized successful Feishu/Lark JSON responses**: they now fail closed at 16 MiB instead of being read unbounded.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? **No**
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? **No** — the same endpoints, headers, and token cache semantics are used.
- What is the highest-risk area? Maintainer acceptance of a 16 MiB fail-closed cap on Feishu control-plane JSON responses.
- How is that risk mitigated? These responses are control-plane/token/app metadata payloads and should be tiny relative to 16 MiB; under-cap and existing streaming-card tests verify normal behavior remains intact.

## Current review state

- **Next action:** ClawSweeper re-review, then maintainer review if CI stays green.
- **Waiting on:** CI after rebase/push.
- **Bot/reviewer comments addressed:** Resolved merge conflict; covered the sibling `streaming-card.ts` Feishu token/card-create JSON readers; changed the Feishu wrapper to delegate to shared `readProviderJsonResponse`; kept upstream QR rendering test.

target.title: fix(google-meet): bound Calendar and Meet API response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## Summary

- Bounds the Google Calendar/Meet successful JSON reads in `calendar.ts` and `meet.ts` with the shared 16 MiB `readProviderJsonResponse` cap.
- Covers Calendar `events.list`, Meet `spaces.get`, Meet `spaces.create`, and the shared paginated Meet API helper used by conference records, participants, recordings, transcripts, and smart notes.
- Adds proof through two layers: direct controlled `Response` tests and a real `fetchWithSsrFGuard` path with a mocked global fetch.

## Linked context

No linked issue; this is a narrow bounded-response-read hardening follow-up for the Google Meet plugin.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Four Google Calendar/Meet success-path `response.json()` reads could buffer an unbounded response body. This PR routes them through the shared provider JSON reader, which fails closed at 16 MiB.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux x86-64, local OpenClaw repo environment, latest `upstream/main` base, production Google Meet/Calendar functions, production `readProviderJsonResponse`, and production `fetchWithSsrFGuard` in the new fetch-guard proof test.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules \
    node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/google-meet/src/calendar.test.ts extensions/google-meet/src/meet.test.ts extensions/google-meet/src/api-fetch-guard.test.ts -- --run
  PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules \
    /media/vdc/0668001470/workSpace/claw-campaign/openclaw/node_modules/.bin/oxlint \
    extensions/google-meet/src/calendar.ts extensions/google-meet/src/meet.ts \
    extensions/google-meet/src/calendar.test.ts extensions/google-meet/src/meet.test.ts \
    extensions/google-meet/src/api-fetch-guard.test.ts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```text
  [google-meet fetch-guard proof] calendar events via real fetch guard: count=1
  [google-meet fetch-guard proof] Meet spaces oversized JSON canceled at 16842752/17825792 bytes
  Test Files  3 passed (3)
  Tests  9 passed (9)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** Under-cap Calendar events still return a selected Meet URI through the real fetch guard path. An oversized Meet `spaces.get` JSON body is rejected with `google-meet.spaces.get: JSON response exceeds ... bytes` and the stream is cancelled before full buffering. Existing controlled-response tests also cover Calendar over-cap, Meet create-space over-cap, under-cap parsing, malformed/negative-control behavior, and release calls.
- **What was not tested:** Live Google Calendar/Meet API calls with real OAuth credentials. No Google Workspace credentials are available in this environment.
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** The new proof does not hit Google's network. It intentionally lets production `fetchWithSsrFGuard` run hostname policy/header/release logic while `globalThis.fetch` is mocked to return controlled real `Response` bodies, so credentials and external services are not required.
- **Before evidence:** Current main used bare `response.json()` in Calendar/Meet success paths. The previous PR version only mocked `fetchWithSsrFGuard`; this update adds proof through the real guard path.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/google-meet/src/calendar.test.ts extensions/google-meet/src/meet.test.ts extensions/google-meet/src/api-fetch-guard.test.ts -- --run` — 3 files, 9 tests passed.
- `oxlint extensions/google-meet/src/calendar.ts extensions/google-meet/src/meet.ts extensions/google-meet/src/calendar.test.ts extensions/google-meet/src/meet.test.ts extensions/google-meet/src/api-fetch-guard.test.ts` — 0 problems; rerun after fixing CI `typescript(no-base-to-string)` in the fetch-guard proof test.

## Risk checklist

- Did user-visible behavior change? **Only for oversized successful Google Calendar/Meet JSON responses**: they now fail closed at 16 MiB instead of buffering unbounded.
- Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? **No**
- Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? **No** — same endpoints, scopes, and headers are used.
- What is the highest-risk area? Maintainer acceptance of a 16 MiB cap for Calendar/Meet API JSON responses.
- How is that risk mitigated? Google API responses here are paginated/control-plane payloads; the new real-guard under-cap test verifies normal request shape and parsing, while over-cap tests verify cancellation.

## Current review state

- **Next action:** ClawSweeper re-review, then maintainer review if CI stays green.
- **Waiting on:** CI after push.
- **Bot/reviewer comments addressed:** Added stronger real fetch-guard path proof; fixed CI lint by avoiding default stringification of `RequestInfo`; no production code changes beyond the original bounded-reader swap.

target.title: Redact exec tool result payloads
target.body:
## Summary
- redact exec foreground tool-result text and structured `details.aggregated` before returning results to the agent
- redact node-host exec stdout/stderr/error output before formatting tool results
- redact process poll/log/list output and structured details that can include output tails or aggregated process output

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Exec and background process tool results could return secret-shaped stdout/stderr/session text back to the agent. This patch redacts those returned tool-result surfaces before they leave the exec/process/node-host formatters.

**Real environment tested:** Real local OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/exec-tool-result-redaction` at `da701a0bbf6c`, using an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME`. The proof used an obvious fake canary value, not a real credential.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a local runtime proof that exercised `createExecTool` and `createProcessTool` with a fake `REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET` value, then inspected the returned tool-result `content[0].text` and structured `details.aggregated` values for foreground exec, process poll, process log, and process list tail/details surfaces.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live output from the local runtime proof:

```json
{
  "foregroundExec": {
    "status": "completed",
    "text": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "detailsAggregated": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  },
  "processPoll": {
    "status": "completed",
    "text": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890\n\n\nProcess exited with code 0.",
    "detailsAggregated": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890\n",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  },
  "processLog": {
    "status": "completed",
    "text": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "detailsAggregated": "",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  },
  "processList": {
    "status": "completed",
    "textTail": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  }
}
```

Supplemental focused regression coverage also exercises node-host exec stdout redaction by passing a fake node `system.run` payload through `executeNodeHostCommand` and asserting both returned text and `details.aggregated` redact the raw canary.

**Observed result after fix:** The raw fake canary value was absent from returned foreground exec, process poll, process log, and process list output. Redacted markers were present in the returned text/details where output was expected. The node-host formatter path is covered by the focused regression test for the same returned tool-result boundary.

**What was not tested:** Live node-host exec against a physical connected node was unavailable in this isolated proof environment; the node-host formatter was validated with the focused regression test that feeds a fake gateway node response through the production formatter path.

## Tests
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/agents/bash-tools.exec.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node-phases.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped stdout'`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped output'`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped node stdout'`

Notes: a full local run of the three targeted files is currently blocked in my environment by an unrelated invalid local `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` model alias (`openai-codex/gpt-5.3-codex`) during the pre-existing foreground timeout test. The new redaction regression tests pass individually under the agents-core Vitest config.

Fixes #71211



## Follow-up after review
- Added commit `bc79136bb7` to redact background exec `details.tail` before returning running-session tool results.
- Added regression coverage for background exec `details.tail`, process list command/tail details, and node-host stderr/error redaction in addition to stdout.

Additional local verification after follow-up:
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped node'`
- `git diff --check`

Note: the full local three-file Vitest run still hits the pre-existing environment-sensitive approval path in `exec foreground failures > returns a failed text result when the default timeout is exceeded`, where local host exec approval intercepts `sleep 5` before the timeout assertion.

## Review feedback update - 2026-05-21
- Addressed ClawSweeper P1 by removing the JSON embedded-fallback behavior change from this PR; `openclaw agent --json` fallback behavior is back to the existing contract.
- Addressed ClawSweeper P2 by replacing the live-update process timing test with deterministic `runExecProcess` regression coverage, so the previous single-line skip branches are gone.
- Final head verified: `8793847f206cca93c49abe33e4f49b9b55a0c4dc`.

Additional verification on final head:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped stdout in update text and details tail'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts -t 'JSON'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped node'`\n- `pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.test.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts src/cli/program/register.agent.ts src/cli/program/register.agent.test.ts`\n- `git diff --check && git diff --cached --check`\n\nChanged-file gate note: `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"` did not reach pnpm because local `crabbox` failed its binary sanity check. Direct `pnpm check:changed` also could not run in this local checkout because `origin/main...HEAD` has no merge base on the grafted/shallow branch. The focused tests, lint, and diff checks above passed on the final head.

## Process control redaction update - 2026-05-21
- Addressed ClawSweeper P1 by redacting command-derived process result names for running-session controls (`write`, `submit`, `paste`) plus `kill`, `remove`, `poll`, `log`, `list`, and `send-keys`.
- Added fake-secret regression coverage for `process write` and `process send-keys` command-derived `details.name` values.
- Final head verified locally: `24ac21f4280e9e403722c271d12fda0a4c68535d4`.

Additional verification on final head:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.process-send-keys.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped stdout in update text and details tail'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped node'`\n- `pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/bash-tools.process.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process-send-keys.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process-send-keys.test.ts`\n- `git diff --check && git diff --cached --check`

target.title: feat(mxc): add Windows MXC sandbox backend
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw does not currently have an official installable MXC sandbox backend for Windows hosts that can run sandboxed commands through Microsoft eXecution Containers. This leaves MXC-capable Windows environments without a first-party plugin path for ProcessContainer-backed execution and filesystem bridge operations.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds an official OpenShell-style external `mxc` sandbox plugin package (`@openclaw/mxc-sandbox`) with Windows-only readiness checks, plugin-owned MXC SDK dependency ownership, ProcessContainer payload construction, a plugin-side launcher, host-backed filesystem bridge support, sandbox policy loading, and targeted tests.

The plugin source lives under `extensions/mxc`, but it is packaged as an installable plugin (`bundledDist: false`) so the MXC SDK/native dependency is owned by the plugin package instead of an unshipped bundled `node_modules` tree. The backend uses the stable current MXC `0.7.0-alpha` schema and keeps sandbox defaults conservative: curated environments, headless UI, per-exec temp directories, absolute binary overrides, host-derived Windows roots, read-only default workspace access for `none`/`ro`, and fail-closed unsupported policy handling.

## User Impact

Users on MXC-capable Windows hosts can install the official MXC sandbox plugin, restart the Gateway, configure `sandbox.backend: "mxc"`, and run OpenClaw sandboxed commands through MXC. Non-Windows or unsupported Windows hosts remain unaffected because the plugin stays dormant when readiness probes fail.

Install command:

```bash
openclaw plugins install @openclaw/mxc-sandbox
```

## Evidence

- `pnpm test:extension mxc -- --reporter=verbose` — passed.
- `pnpm test extensions\mxc test\scripts\generate-npm-shrinkwrap.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` — passed.
- `pnpm test:changed` — passed.
- `pnpm release:plugins:npm:check -- --plugins @openclaw/mxc-sandbox` — passed; publishable plugin metadata looks OK.
- `pnpm release:plugins:clawhub:check -- --plugins @openclaw/mxc-sandbox` — passed; publishable plugin metadata looks OK.
- `pnpm check` — passed, including import cycle check with 0 runtime value cycles.
- `pnpm build` — passed.
- `node scripts\lib\plugin-npm-runtime-build.mjs extensions\mxc` plus package-manifest `npm pack --dry-run` — passed; package includes `dist/index.js`, `dist/mxc-spawn-launcher.mjs`, and MXC SDK binaries including `node_modules/@microsoft/mxc-sdk/bin/x64/wxc-exec.exe`.
- `pnpm deps:changes:report -- --base-ref origin/main --markdown <session>/dependency-changes.md --json <session>/dependency-changes.json` — generated dependency report.

Real Windows MXC package proof:

- Crabbox provider: `ssh`
- Lease/id: `static_10-127-3-94`
- Target: `windows-normal`
- Remote Node/npm: `node v26.4.0`, `npm 11.17.0`
- Built and packed `@openclaw/mxc-sandbox@2026.6.10` on the Windows host.
- Installed published `openclaw@2026.6.10` plus the local packed plugin in a fresh temp project.
- Verified package-owned runtime dependency path:
  - `PROOF:installedOpenClaw=2026.6.10`
  - `PROOF:installedPlugin=2026.6.10`
  - `PROOF:mxcSdk=0.7.0`
  - `PROOF:wxcExists=True ...\node_modules\@openclaw\mxc-sandbox\node_modules\@microsoft\mxc-sdk\bin\x64\wxc-exec.exe`
- Registered and ran backend `mxc`:
  - `PROOF:registeredServices=mxc-sandbox-cleanup`
  - `PROOF:backendId=mxc`
  - sandbox command: `echo MXC_PROOF_OK && cd`
  - `PROOF:exit=0`
  - `PROOF:stdout=MXC_PROOF_OK |...\workspace|`
  - `PROOF:stderr=`

User-facing plugin install proof on the same Windows host:

- Installed published `openclaw@2026.6.10` in an isolated temp project.
- Ran `node <published-openclaw>/dist/entry.js plugins install <local @openclaw/mxc-sandbox tgz> --force --pin`.
- Install succeeded:
  - `PROOF3:installExit=0`
  - install output included `Installing plugin dependencies…`, `Installed plugin: mxc`, and `Restart the gateway to load plugins.`
  - `PROOF3:configExists=True`
  - `PROOF3:configHasMxc=True`
  - `PROOF3:pluginRoot=...\state\extensions\mxc`
  - `PROOF3:installedPlugin=2026.6.10`

Workspace access proof after review 4588711229:

- Static SSH Windows host: `provider=ssh`, lease `static_10-127-3-94`, target `windows-normal`.
- `workspaceAccess: "none"` now keeps the MXC workdir read-only rather than read-write by default:
  - `PROOF4:backendId=mxc`
  - `PROOF4:workspaceAccess=none`
  - `PROOF4:workdirReadonly=true`
  - `PROOF4:workdirReadwrite=false`
- Command execution still succeeds with the read-only default workspace policy:
  - sandbox command: `echo MXC_NONE_PROOF_OK && cd`
  - `PROOF4:exit=0`
  - `PROOF4:stdout=MXC_NONE_PROOF_OK |...\workspace|`
  - `PROOF4:stderr=`

Full `pnpm test` was attempted earlier per CONTRIBUTING, but the broad local suite hit unrelated existing non-MXC failures in UI/TUI/agent runtime tests before completion. The changed/MXC-specific lanes above passed cleanly.

Copilot autoreview was attempted on the latest review-fix head. It produced a finding about `@microsoft/mxc-sdk@0.7.0` re-serializing config into `--config-base64` for `wxc-exec`; local source inspection confirmed that this is the current upstream SDK transport behavior and that the SDK exposes no non-argv transport for `spawnSandboxFromConfig`. No local OpenClaw-only patch is available without an upstream SDK/executor transport change.

## Notes

- Contributor changelog edit was removed per CONTRIBUTING.
- `.github/labeler.yml` maps `extensions/mxc/**` to `plugin: mxc`.
- Upstream label verification found no existing `plugin: mxc` label; label creation requires maintainer permissions.
- Dependency Graph Guard still requires maintainer/security `/allow-dependencies-change` approval for the current head SHA 7dc546ccb7ffc27830bbe1c932f58640662c52c7.

target.title: fix(gateway): gate cron on channel readiness
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Prevent gateway cron from starting delivery work before configured channel transports report ready during startup and hot reload.

Why does this matter now?

- Cron catch-up can immediately deliver notifications after gateway startup or config reload; Discord can be running before it is connected.

What is the intended outcome?

- Cron waits for running configured channel accounts with explicit `connected` state, then fail-opens after the existing channel connect grace timeout with a warning.
- Hot reload restarts cron after channel restart completes, and restores the old cron if channel restart fails after the old cron was stopped.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- No plugin changes, config changes, migrations, or delivery retry policy changes.

What does success look like?

- Startup and hot-reload cron paths do not race known channel readiness, without blocking disabled, unconfigured, stopped, skipped, or closing gateways.

What should reviewers focus on?

- `src/gateway/server-runtime-services.ts` and `src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.ts` lifecycle ordering around cron start, channel readiness, skipped channels, and rollback on channel restart failure.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #62891

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Maintainer-requested local fix pass.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: gateway cron startup and hot-reload cron restart now wait for running configured channel accounts with explicit `connected` state before starting cron, with timeout fail-open and skipped-channel bypasses.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, Node runtime executing the gateway startup and hot-reload paths from this branch.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/cron-channel-readiness.test.ts src/gateway/server-runtime-services.test.ts src/gateway/server-reload-handlers.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-cron.test.ts src/cron/service.restart-catchup.test.ts src/gateway/server-import-boundary.test.ts
```

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) - **Behavior or issue addressed**: Pin-from-here mirrors a session turn into pinned target threads by re-running each target channel's own dispatch with a bus-sourced reply resolver, so the mirror renders + persists natively (streaming/verbose per that channel's own config) instead of a flat post-hoc echo. - **Real environment tested**: A real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch in a Linux crabbox with a desktop; a real Telegram bot + a real Telegram account on Telegram Desktop; a real `claude-cli` backend (`sonnet-4-6`/`opus-4-8`, live Anthropic API) with exec tools; the tdlib user-driver sending real messages. This recreates the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow locally (same mechanism: telegram-desktop + user-driver + screen capture). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Started the gateway from the built branch; in a Telegram thread sent `/new`, `/verbose on`, `/pin on`; then drove the same session from webchat (`chat.send`) with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname`, and a two-step `date -u` + `uname -a` narrated before each command); screen-recorded Telegram Desktop and captured the gateway debug log. - **Evidence after fix**: Direct after-fix artifacts — mirror-render screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png), multi-account screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png), and the full **destination-revocation run + gateway log** [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The revocation log shows the mirror deliver when enabled (`messageId=0` synthetic inbound + `outbound send ok chatId=…`), then `[outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed … (destination disabled/revoked)` after the group is disabled. - **Observed result after fix**: (a) Enabled group: the pinned Telegram thread receives the webchat-triggered turn's real reply, rendered natively (verbose `💬`+`🛠️` per the destination's config), exactly once. (b) **Disabled (revoked) group**: zero sends to the group and the tdlib driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE` — neither the native mirror nor the echo fallback delivers. Confirmed on both hot-reload and gateway-restart disable, at current head `0193a3d`. - **What was not tested**: Nothing material remains unproven on current head. **Destination revocation is now proven live** (and a real gap it surfaced is fixed — see *Destination revocation* below). Multi-account routing is also proven on current head — see below. #### Multi-account routing — current head (account-aware dispatch) Two real Telegram accounts in one gateway: `default` = bot #2 in GID `-1003900553563`, `acct2` = bot #3 (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot`) in a separate forum group GID2 `-1004389798714` (bot #3 promoted admin; bot #2 is **not** a member of GID2). The echo target for GID2 is set with `accountId: acct2`, then a **webchat-origin** turn drives the session. Gateway debug log (`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount-log.txt`): ```text [telegram] [acct2] starting provider (@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot) [telegram] [default] starting provider (@JeeevesOpenClawTelegram2bot) message received: channel=webchat ... sessionKey=agent:main:telegram:group:-1004389798714:topic:1 message dispatch started: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver ← the mirror message dispatch completed: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver outcome=completed ``` The webchat-origin turn mirrored into GID2 (`source=replyResolver` = the bus-sourced mirror), with **zero** `mirror: no dispatcher` fail-closed warnings (the acct2 dispatcher resolved). Because **bot #2 is not in GID2** (`getChatMember` → `chat not found`), a successful mirror delivery there could *only* be the **acct2** account (bot #3) — proving the mirror routes through the target's own account, not the first-registered/default one. #### Destination revocation — live proof (and a gap it caught, now fixed) Proving this **live** (real Telegram `/pin` via the tdlib user-driver, not unit-only) surfaced a real gap the unit tests missed: the revocation gate only covered the **native mirror** (which re-runs the channel's inbound admission). The prompt / post-hoc **echo** path (`fireEchoDeliveries`) delivers through the *channel-agnostic* raw send, which has **no** admission gate — so a disabled pinned group kept receiving echoes, and when the mirror fan-out didn't fire (e.g. right after the channel restart that disabling a group triggers) the post-hoc echo leaked the conversation to the revoked destination. **Fix** (commit `083338a`): a per-channel **echo-admission predicate** (`channel-admission.ts`), registered per account exactly like the mirror dispatcher (last-wins, unregister on stop, **fail closed**). `fireEchoDeliveries` filters targets through it; telegram registers a predicate that reports group/topic-disabled from its live config. Now a disabled destination gets **neither** the native mirror **nor** the echo fallback. **Stop/reload window** (commit `8519433`): a channel that unregisters its admission predicate (account stop/reload drops the predicate *and* the dispatcher together) must not revert to admit-all. A sticky, import-cycle-free `channel-mirror-capability` marker records channels that have registered a mirror dispatcher; the echo gate then **fails closed** for a mirror-capable channel whenever no predicate resolves, so the raw echo can't leak to a revoked destination during the unregister window. Non-mirror channels admit echoes unchanged. Unit-covered (`channel-admission.test.ts`). **Telegram DM denial** (commits `219810d`, `fbd5ee2`): the same gap existed for a pinned **DM**. Three parts: (a) `enforceTelegramDmAccess` denial returned null *without* the revocation marker → `dispatchMirror` threw → unmark → raw echo could reach the denied DM; now a denied DM in a mirror context calls `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked` (keep the mark, suppress the post-hoc). (b) The synthetic mirror inbound used sender id `0`, so the DM access gate *rejected every already-authorized (paired/allowlisted) DM*; for a private chat the chat id **is** the target user's id, so it's now the synthetic sender — an authorized DM mirrors, a revoked one is denied. (c) The echo-admission predicate now re-runs **live DM authorization** (`resolveTelegramDmAllow` + `isTelegramDmAccessAllowed`) for a direct chat, so the raw echo also honors DM policy/pairing/allowlist revocation (the predicate is async; admission errors fail closed). Unit-covered: `bot-message-context.mirror.test.ts` (DM-denial → `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked`), `channel-admission.test.ts` (async predicate + fail-closed), `echo.test.ts` (async suppression). **Live-proven** (real DM: driver/Cameron DMs bot #2, `/pin on` in the DM): with `dmPolicy: open` the authorized DM mirrors (validating the sender-identity fix); after `dmPolicy: disabled` the DM gets **zero** sends, the driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`, and the gateway logs `Echo delivery suppressed … telegram:8661849123 (destination disabled/revoked)` — see the *DIRECT-DM REVOCATION* section of [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). **Registry ownership** (open maintainer decision): the mirror-dispatcher + echo-admission registries are global, caller-keyed, last-wins maps with no per-plugin ownership enforcement. They are exported from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-outbound` and **only the bundled telegram channel uses them**. Whether to keep them as a public seam, hand them out through an owner-scoped registrar, or keep them internal is a maintainer API call — flagged explicitly in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`. (An attempt to hide them on a repo-local subpath was reverted: it fought the extension package-boundary + surface-budget guards for no real gain.) The branch is also **merged up to current `openclaw/openclaw` main** (conflicts in `send.ts` / `agent-runner-execution.ts` resolved). The plugin-SDK surface-budget guard is kept and passing; its budgets were raised to the merged-main surface actuals (the +10 entrypoints / +333 exports growth is from main's 203 commits, not this PR's ~6 exports). **Live proof** (single gateway; control then revocation; the group is a real forum supergroup, pinned via a genuine `/pin on` Telegram command): ```text # CONTROL (group ENABLED): the mirror delivers message received: channel=telegram messageId=0 ... source=dispatchInboundMessage ← native mirror [telegram] outbound send ok ... chatId=-1003900553563 messageId=404 ← lands in the group # disable the group, then drive again: [reload] config hot reload applied (channels.telegram.groups.-1003900553563.enabled) [outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed for telegram:telegram:-1003900553563 (destination disabled/revoked) ``` - **Before the fix:** the driver observed `📱 [via webchat] …` arriving in the disabled group (+1 send). - **After the fix:** `sends-to-GID after revoke = control count (0 added)`, and the **tdlib driver observing the group reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`** — the disabled destination receives nothing. Confirmed on **both** revocation paths: config hot-reload **and** a full gateway restart. Unit coverage: `channel-admission.test.ts` (registry + fail-closed) and an `echo.test.ts` suppression case. Full redacted run + gateway log for both paths: [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The same run filmed in Telegram Desktop — the bot rendering into GID2 is **"Jeeves OC Test Account 2" (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot` = acct2)**, mirroring the webchat-origin turn (`📱 [via webchat] …`) and its reply natively: Account-aware dispatch also fails closed on an **explicit account mismatch** (a target pinning a different account than the only registered one is never mirrored through the wrong account) — unit-regressed. Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Public SDK contract The mirror-dispatcher seam (`registerChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `unregisterChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `MirrorDispatcher`) is now **documented** as a public `channel-outbound` contract in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md` (new *Channel mirror dispatcher* section): per-account registration, the last-wins reload lifecycle, and the account-match + destination-revocation behavior a channel plugin must honor (the native dispatcher is the sole delivery authority, so it must run the channel's own admission/revocation path). The barrel export points at that section. It stays on its neutral SDK subpath per the repo's internal-module convention rather than being reached into as an internal — final acceptance of the public surface is the maintainers' call. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

commands.review_summary:
The PR makes Telegram empty rendered text sends resolve as skipped no-ops and updates reply accounting/tests so skipped chunks are not counted as delivered.

PR surface: Source +81, Tests +330. Total +411 across 5 files.

Reproducibility: yes. by source inspection and report evidence. Current main still only matches the legacy empty-text wording, while the original user report and PR body show Telegram returning `text must be non-empty`.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Needs contributor or maintainer rebase plus redacted exact-head live Telegram proof; automation cannot supply the contributor's real transport proof for this external PR.

target.title: fix(telegram): silently skip empty-text sends
target.body:
Supersedes #59725. The original PR was auto-closed on 2026-04-02 by openclaw-barnacle for the 10-open-PR cap. Reopening it programmatically fails because the close `stateReason` is `COMPLETED` plus the branch was force-pushed since, so this is a fresh PR carrying the rebased fix. ## Behavior addressed Interrupted mid-reply turns can produce formatted Telegram HTML that has no visible text and no plain-text fallback, for example an empty supported tag such as ``. Telegram rejects that payload with HTTP 400 (`message text is empty` legacy wording, `text must be non-empty` newer wording). Previously this surfaced as a user-visible delivery failure; this PR silently treats that empty-text send as a no-op. Parse errors remain out of scope and still throw. Live Telegram proof below shows bare `
` payloads currently fail as parse errors, while ` ` payloads are accepted by Telegram and still deliver normally. ## Change - `sendTelegramText` now returns `Promise` so it can report a skipped no-op send without inventing a Telegram message id. - When formatted HTML trims empty and the plain fallback also trims empty, `sendTelegramText` skips before calling Telegram. - When Telegram returns an empty-text 400 and there is no plain fallback, `sendTelegramText` logs verbosely and returns `undefined`. - Unrelated Telegram failures and parse errors still throw unchanged. - Reply accounting now ignores `undefined` send results, so skipped chunks do not advance reply state, delivered count, transcript mirroring, `message_sent.success`, or pinning. - Voice fallback chunking keeps reply/quote/button state available for the next real delivered chunk after a skipped empty-text chunk. ## Live Telegram round-trip proof Driver script `tsx`-imported the source-tree `sendTelegramText` from this PR branch and drove it through a real grammY `Bot` against the live Telegram Bot API. Token and chat id were redacted. Raw Telegram baseline: ``` payload="
" expected=parse-error -> error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br\" at byte offset 0" payload="
" expected=parse-error -> error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br/\" at byte offset 0" payload="
" expected=parse-error -> error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br\" at byte offset 0" payload=" " expected=accepted-by-telegram -> OK message_id=10657 payload="   " expected=accepted-by-telegram -> OK message_id=10658 payload="" expected=empty-text -> error_code=400 description="Bad Request: text must be non-empty" ``` Source-tree `sendTelegramText(bot, chatId, payload, runtime, { textMode: "html", plainText: "" })`: ``` payload="
" expected=parse-error -> THREW error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br\" at byte offset 0" payload="
" expected=parse-error -> THREW error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br/\" at byte offset 0" payload="
" expected=parse-error -> THREW error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br\" at byte offset 0" payload=" " expected=accepted-by-telegram -> resolved=10659 payload="   " expected=accepted-by-telegram -> resolved=10660 payload="" expected=empty-text -> resolved=undefined ``` A well-formed payload still delivered with real Telegram `message_id=10661`. ## Tests ``` node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/bot node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --only=extensions --threads=4 env -u OPENAI_API_KEY -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY pnpm ui:i18n:check node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.ui.config.ts --reporter=verbose pnpm build PATH="$HOME/.opengrep/cli/latest:$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH" OPENCLAW_OPENGREP_BASE_REF=upstream/main...HEAD scripts/run-opengrep.sh --changed --sarif --error ``` The i18n check was run with provider keys unset to match CI fallback-only behavior; with local Anthropic credentials present, the script tried to refresh pending translations and failed on fenced JSON from the provider, which is unrelated to this PR. ## Real behavior proof Behavior addressed: Empty visible-text Telegram HTML sends with empty plain fallback no longer surface as delivery failures; they are treated as no-op sends while unrelated Telegram parse errors still throw. Real environment tested: Live Telegram Bot API using a real grammY Bot; bot token and chat id were redacted from the captured output. Exact steps or command run after this patch: A source-tree driver imported sendTelegramText from this PR branch and called sendTelegramText(bot, chatId, payload, runtime, { textMode: "html", plainText: "" }) for bare
variants,   payloads, an empty supported tag, and a normal message. Evidence after fix: Copied live output from the source-tree driver: ```text Source-tree sendTelegramText(bot, chatId, payload, runtime, { textMode: "html", plainText: "" }) against live Telegram Bot API: payload="
" expected=parse-error -> THREW error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br\" at byte offset 0" payload="
" expected=parse-error -> THREW error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br/\" at byte offset 0" payload="
" expected=parse-error -> THREW error_code=400 description="Bad Request: can't parse entities: Unsupported start tag \"br\" at byte offset 0" payload=" " expected=accepted-by-telegram -> resolved=10659 payload="   " expected=accepted-by-telegram -> resolved=10660 payload="" expected=empty-text -> resolved=undefined well-formed payload -> delivered with real Telegram message_id=10661 ``` Observed result after fix: Empty-text sends skip without incrementing reply delivery state; accepted Telegram payloads still deliver; bare unsupported
HTML still throws the Telegram parse error and remains out of scope. What was not tested: This proof did not exercise every channel or a full live interrupted-agent session; focused regression tests cover reply accounting, transcript mirroring, hooks, pin behavior, voice fallback chunking, and Telegram send behavior.
